topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt impossibly heavy in my palm as I turned the lock. Every muscle in my jaw was tight, a knot of sheer panic coiling in my chest. This was it. Back in the house, under the same roof as Mom and Dad. I smoothed down the front of my shirt for the tenth time, a futile gesture against the sudden, intense heat rising on my neck. When I finally pushed the door open, the silence of the entryway seemed amplified, pressing in on me. My gaze darted everywhere—the perfectly polished floor, the framed family photos—before settling on my parents, who were standing near the archway, a warm, welcoming smile plastered on their faces. I managed a small, jerky nod, my throat suddenly dry. "Hi," I managed, the sound thin and reedy. I wanted the floor to swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I kept my eyes fixed on the worn pattern of the rug beneath my feet, willing myself to become invisible. The air in the living room was thick with unspoken history, and I felt like an intruder, a misplaced piece of furniture in this familiar scene. Each breath I took felt shallow, like I was struggling to pull air through a constricted space. When my mother turned, her expression was one of gentle surprise, and the sudden focus of her attention made my cheeks burn. I mumbled something incoherent, something about needing a glass of water, and immediately took a step backward, my heel catching on the edge of the coffee table. The scrape of my shoe sounded deafening in the quiet room. I couldn't meet anyone’s eyes; I focused instead on the intricate details of the molding on the ceiling, desperate for a distraction that didn't involve facing them.

[story 3]

It was the way the light hit the antique furniture that made everything feel too sharp, too exposed. I clutched the strap of my backpack so tightly my knuckles were white, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to something solid. Walking into the kitchen felt like navigating a minefield. My father was setting out a plate of cookies, his movements slow and unhurried, and I could feel the scrutiny of his gaze, even if he wasn't looking directly at me. I managed a strained, overly bright smile that felt like it was cracking on my face. "Everything's... fine," I said, my voice pitched several octaves too high. I immediately started fiddling with the cuff of my sweater, my hands trembling slightly as I tried to appear casual, utterly failing to hide the frantic energy vibrating just beneath my skin.
